exemplar.title: Investigating the change rate in temperature during temperature rising of electrified metals

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigating the change rate in temperature during temperature rising of electrified metals

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Introduces the topic with clear background on resistivity and Joule heating, giving the reader an understandable context.
States a research question and outlines a systematic procedure with materials, variables, and repeated trials.
Presents a coherent method section that generally aligns with standard experimental structure.
weaknesses: Title and research question remain too broad and omit key parameters, weakening the investigationâs focus.
Methodology fails to control voltage and does not measure resistance directly, undermining coherence with the stated aims.
Key methodological detailsâsuch as uncertainty in supplied voltageâare missing, limiting the rigor and completeness of the approach.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work communicates the topic in an adequate but not fully precise manner (mid-band performance) and provides a mostly complete methodology with relevant elements. However, the broad RQ, gaps in control of variables and missing resistance measurements prevent it from achieving a higher score. A score of 3/6 reflects this solid yet unrefined execution.

strengths: Demonstrates a sound grasp of core physics concepts such as resistivity, resistanceâtemperature relationships, and Jouleâs law.
Applies relevant equations and uses key terminology (resistivity, resistance, specific heat) in context.
Shows clear understanding of qualitative behavior of temperature rise in metals under current.
weaknesses: Occasional misuses of notation (e.g. W=VÃA instead of VÃI) and conflation of resistivity with resistance reduce technical accuracy.
Terminology such as âelectrifying voltageâ and inconsistent variable naming impede precision.
Equations are quoted without substantive application to experimental data, limiting depth of conceptual linkage.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student shows good subject knowledge and generally accurate use of terminology (placing it in the 3â4 band), but imprecise language, occasional conceptual oversimplifications, and limited quantitative application of formulae prevent achievement of an excellent level. A score of 3/6 appropriately reflects this competent yet uneven performance.

strengths: Processes primary data into averages, uncertainties, slopes and linear fits, demonstrating clear engagement with analysis.
Identifies trends and anomalies (e.g. fluctuating rates vs. hypothesis) and links findings back to the research question.
Structures a logical argument progressing from background through data to conclusions and evaluates sources of error.
weaknesses: Error analysis remains superficial; uncertainties are listed but not propagated or quantitatively linked to conclusions.
Evaluation of methodological limitations is descriptive rather than critically quantitative, missing deeper implications of heat losses and variable power input.
Theoryâdata connections (e.g. Q=IÂ²Rt) are cited but not applied to reinforce or challenge experimental results.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis is coherent and clearly relevant to the research question (mid-band 4â6), and the argument is well structured. However, the critical evaluation lacks quantitative depth and the linkage between theory and data is underdeveloped. A mid-range score of 6/12 reflects solid performance in analysis and argument but limited critical evaluation.

strengths: Follows a clear organizational structure with title page, table of contents, section headings, in-text citations and a reference list.
Supports communication with appropriately numbered figures and tables integrated into the text.
Consistently numbered pages and inclusion of word count enhance navigability and adherence to conventions.
weaknesses: Visuals suffer from inconsistent resolution and unit labeling; graphs use mixed time units and omit error bars.
Citation style and formatting of equations and units are inconsistent, detracting from coherence.
Duplicate captions and numbering errors in procedural steps interrupt the logical flow and clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The reportâs structure is sound and generally follows extended-essay conventions, but notable inconsistencies in layout, figure clarity, unit usage and citation formatting limit effectiveness. A score of 3/4 reflects this clear yet uneven presentation.

strandSelections.reasoning: Key physics termsâresistivity, resistance, Joule heating, specific heat capacityâare employed and usually used correctly, indicating reasonable conceptual command.  Nonetheless, imprecise language ("electrifying voltage", "change rate in temperature") and occasional grammatical errors reduce clarity.  Derivations are limited to quoting standard formulae with minimal contextualisation, so terminology use is competent but not consistently precise or sophisticated, aligning with the 3-4 band.

A logical progression from background to hypothesis, data presentation, conclusion, and evaluation is evident, and the student makes an explicit comparison between copper and aluminium.  Nevertheless, the argument relies heavily on descriptive narrative; reasoning is sometimes circular (e.g., concluding resistance changed because temperature changed without measurement) and the rejection of the hypothesis is not followed by deeper alternative explanations.  Coherence is moderate, with some repetition and linguistic imprecision, thus fitting the lower end of the adequate (4-6) band.

The student outlines materials, apparatus, variables, data-collection intervals, and provides trial repetition, demonstrating an awareness of systematic procedure.  Nevertheless, key methodological weaknesses remain: voltage (and thus power input) is allowed to vary uncontrolled; resistanceâcentral to the investigationâis not measured directly; and justification of chosen time points and current level is superficial.  These gaps limit completeness and the capacity of the method to address the research question fully, situating performance in the adequate (mid) band rather than the top.

The essay shows a generally sound grasp of core physics concepts such as resistivity, resistanceâtemperature relationships, and Jouleâs law. Relevant equations are quoted and basic explanations are offered for lattice vibrations and heat generation.  However, application is often qualitative, and the inability to link measured temperature changes to calculated electrical energy or resistance limits the depth of understanding demonstrated.  Occasional misconceptions and oversimplifications (e.g., conflating resistivity and resistance) prevent the work from reaching an excellent level, but the knowledge displayed is more than rudimentary.

Evaluation is present but remains largely superficial: sources of error (probe contact, voltage instability, ambient temperature) are listed with generic remedies; there is little quantitative assessment of their impact, and no consideration of methodological alternatives that could directly measure resistance. Reflection on the validity of the conclusion is limited to restating inconsistencies. This aligns with the characteristics of the 1-3 band for critical evaluation, with a slightly stronger performance warranting the top of that band.

The title and introductory paragraphs convey that the essay will examine how the temperature of copper and aluminium plates changes when a constant current passes through them. Key background ideas (resistivity, Joule heating) are introduced, and a short hypothesis is stated. However, phrasing such as âchange rate in temperature during temperature risingâ is awkward and the purpose is not articulated with complete precision. Important distinctionsâe.g. whether the focus is on rate-of-temperature-rise versus resistance behaviourâremain somewhat blurred. Overall, the topic is understandable but lacks the crisp, unambiguous focus and succinct communication required for a top-band award.

Graphs, tables, and images are present and labelled, supporting communication. Yet several visuals are blurry, axes scales are inconsistent, and some tables/figures repeat information unnecessarily. Formatting of equations, units, and significant figures is uneven; long blocks of text and occasional typographical errors detract from visual clarity. These shortcomings indicate a basic level of layout effectiveness with notable inconsistencies, corresponding to the 1-2 band (score 2).

A single research question is provided, yet it is worded broadly ("Investigating the change rate in temperature during temperature rising of electrified metals") and does not specify key parameters such as material dimensions, current, or how ârateâ will be defined.  While the later sections imply a comparison between copper and aluminium, this comparative aspect is not built into the stated question, and the link between the RQ and subsequent analysis (which centres on temperatureâtime graphs rather than direct resistance measurements) is only partial.  Consequently, the question guides the work only loosely, fitting the lower-middle band.

The student gathers primary data, processes it into averages, uncertainties, slopes, and linear fits, and attempts to interpret R values.  Analysis is directly linked to the (imperfect) research question and identifies trends and anomalies (e.g., fluctuating rates, contrary to hypothesis).  However, the depth of interpretation is limited; key factors such as heat losses, changing power input, and thermal equilibrium are acknowledged only briefly and not quantitatively addressed.  The work thus demonstrates an adequate-to-good level of analysis but lacks the consistency and rigour required for higher bands, meriting a mid-range score within 4-6.

The essay includes required organizational elements: title page with word count, structured table of contents, consistently headed sections, in-text citations, and a reference list. Figures and tables are numbered and generally integrated. Minor issuesâsuch as inconsistent capitalisation, occasional duplication of captions, and some layout clutterâprevent seamless readability, but overall structure adheres to EE conventions, meriting the 3-4 band (awarded 3 to reflect these flaws).

annotations.comment: The caption âFigure 1. Set up materials of experimentâ appears twice, causing redundancy. Each figure should have a single, unique caption.
The experimental setup image is low contrast and slightly blurred, making key apparatus details hard to discern; higherâresolution figures would support clearer communication.
The citation style is inconsistent (journal articles differ from web sources in formatting), reducing presentation coherence. A uniform referencing style should be applied throughout.
The student has provided a word count at the start of the report, fulfilling the formatting requirement and aiding in adherence to the IBâs word-limit guidelines.
The equation is written as $$W = V \times A$$ instead of $$W = V \times I$$, misusing the symbol for current (I). This notation error must be corrected to maintain technical accuracy.
The step numbering in the procedure list has duplicate â3.â entries, disrupting the logical flow. Consistent numbering would improve readability and structure.
The text refers to an âiron plateâ when demonstrating uncertainty calculation, which appears to be an errorâthis should reference the copper or aluminium sample consistently.
The slope formula $$m=\frac{y_2-y_1}{x_2-x_1}$$ is applied without propagating experimental uncertainties; including error analysis here would strengthen the evaluation of trends.
The power equation $$P=I\times V$$ is introduced but not applied to calculate or interpret data, limiting its usefulness in the analysis.
The relationship $$Q=I^2Rt$$ is cited but not linked to the experimental slopes or energy calculations, missing an opportunity to connect theory and data quantitatively.

The title is overly generic and does not specify key parameters such as material dimensions, current value or how âchange rateâ will be quantified. A more precise title would clarify the investigationâs focus and improve coherence.

The research question is stated too broadly and omits critical detailsâsuch as a comparative element between copper and aluminium and the definition of ârateââwhich weakens its guiding function for the investigation.
The introduction asserts that resistance change will be observed, yet the methodology does not include any direct measurement of resistance, undermining the investigationâs coherence.

The methodology admits inability to calculate supplied energy due to unmeasured voltage but proceeds to draw conclusions about energy conversion; without continuous voltage data, these conclusions lack empirical support.
The initial dataâcollection table omits an uncertainty for the supplied voltage; specifying this would enable more accurate propagation of error and stronger analysis.
Uncertainties in the average temperatures reach up to Â±1.9Â°C, indicating poor measurement precision; this variability should be critically analysed and discussed in relation to data reliability.
The table of contents is comprehensive and clear, listing all major sections with corresponding page numbers, which enhances navigability and structure.
The opening sentence conflates resistance and resistivity and features awkward phrasing, reducing conceptual clarity. Precise definitions and correct term usage are needed.
The controlled-variable explanation confuses current and voltage (âelectrifying voltage to be 5Aâ), indicating imprecise use of electrical terminology; this should be corrected for clarity.
Tables of rate of change omit units (e.g., Â°C/s or Â°C/min), making quantitative interpretation unclear; always include appropriate units for derived values.
The discussion summarises fluctuations but stops short of deeper analysisâfactors like heat losses, thermal equilibrium and variable power input should be quantitatively considered.
Graph 1 uses time in minutes, yet earlier data tables use seconds; this inconsistency in units can confuse readers and should be harmonised.
The aluminiumâplate graph repeats the timeâunit inconsistency and lacks labeled error bars, reducing clarity; ensure axes, units and error representations are clear and consistent.
The best-fit line is provided without reporting the coefficient of determination (RÂ²) or uncertainty on the slope, making it hard to assess fit quality rigorously.
The gradient-uncertainty calculation divides the difference of max and min slopes by two without justification; a full propagation of uncertainties would be more robust.
The conclusion restates findings but lacks clear linkage back to the original research question and does not propose avenues for further inquiry or alternative explanations.
The evaluation lists strengths but offers limited critical engagement with how errors quantitatively impacted results. Deeper analysis of error magnitude would strengthen the evaluation.
The limitations table suggests improvements but does not assess how each limitation quantitatively influenced the main findings, missing a critical evaluation opportunity.
